title: Overview
text: Millidyne is a dilution-refrigerator cryostat platform purpose-built for housing superconducting qubit processors, holding a mixing-chamber base temperature low enough for coherence measurements without requiring a dedicated cryogenics specialist on site at every deployment. The platform packages a closed-cycle pulse-tube precooling stage, a helium-3/helium-4 dilution unit, and an integrated vibration-isolation stack into a single delivered system, rather than leaving a customer to assemble those components from separate vendors. Millidyne targets research groups and quantum-computing companies operating between a handful and several hundred qubits, a scale where a fully custom cryostat build is difficult to justify but off-the-shelf laboratory dilution refrigerators were not originally designed with dense qubit wiring and readout hardware in mind. Every design choice in the platform, wiring density, vibration budget, thermal anchoring, is made explicitly in service of preserving qubit coherence rather than optimizing for base temperature alone.

title: Dilution Refrigeration Mechanism
text: Millidyne cools its mixing chamber using the same physical principle as any dilution refrigerator: below roughly 870 millikelvin, a helium-3/helium-4 mixture phase-separates into a helium-3-rich concentrated phase and a dilute phase containing only a small fraction of helium-3. Because dissolving helium-3 into the dilute phase is endothermic, continuously forcing helium-3 across that phase boundary inside the mixing chamber extracts heat, functioning as a refrigeration cycle with no moving parts at the coldest stage. A still, held at a warmer intermediate temperature, evaporates helium-3 preferentially from the dilute phase and returns it to the concentrated phase via external pumping, sustaining the circulation that keeps helium-3 crossing the phase boundary continuously. Millidyne's still-pumping line is sized specifically to sustain adequate circulation at the qubit-relevant heat loads its wiring and readout hardware are expected to impose, rather than the lighter loads typical of a bare, unwired research cryostat.

title: Cooling Stage Specifications
text: Millidyne's standard configuration uses four cooling stages above the mixing chamber: a 50 K stage, a 4 K stage, a still stage held at 0.7 K, and a cold plate at approximately 0.1 K, with the mixing chamber itself reaching a base temperature of 8 millikelvin unloaded. Cooldown from room temperature to base takes 26 hours on the standard configuration, of which roughly 18 hours is spent bringing the 4 K stage down before dilution circulation even begins. The 4 K stage provides up to 1.5 W of cooling power, the still stage up to 12 mW, and the mixing chamber up to 24 microwatts at 100 millikelvin, the reference operating point used across Millidyne's published specification sheets. Total system mass, including the vibration-isolation stack, is 640 kilograms.

title: Vibration Isolation Approach
text: Millidyne isolates the mixing chamber and qubit sample stage from the mechanical vibration its own pulse-tube precooler generates, since pulse-tube coolers achieve high cooling power without moving parts at the coldest stage but still contain a moving displacer at the warm end that couples vibration into the cryostat structure. The platform decouples the pulse-tube cold head from the rest of the cryostat mechanically, connecting it to the 4 K and 50 K stages through flexible copper braids rather than a rigid thermal link, so that pulse-tube vibration is absorbed before reaching the colder stages. A secondary spring-and-mass damping stage suspends the mixing-chamber plate itself, further attenuating any residual vibration that reaches the cold plate through the wiring harness or support structure. Millidyne's isolation stack is tuned specifically against the pulse-tube's fundamental 1.4 Hz operating frequency and its low-order harmonics, the frequencies most likely to couple into flux-sensitive superconducting qubit designs.

title: Thermal Load Budget Statistics
text: A fully wired Millidyne system supporting 256 qubit control and readout lines imposes a measured mixing-chamber heat load of 9.8 microwatts under typical operation, comfortably within the 24-microwatt cooling power budget at the standard 100 millikelvin operating point. Coaxial wiring accounts for 61% of that load, DC control lines for 24%, and radiation and residual gas conduction for the remaining 15%. Still-stage heat load from the same wiring harness averages 3.1 milliwatts, well under the still's 12-milliwatt capacity. Customers exceeding 400 wired lines on the standard configuration are advised to upgrade to Millidyne's high-density wiring kit, which adds attenuated thermalization stages at each cooling plate and has been measured to reduce per-line heat load by 34% relative to the standard harness.

title: Mixing-Chamber Cooling Power
text: The cooling power available at Millidyne's mixing chamber follows the standard dilution-refrigeration relation Qdot = 84 * n3dot * Tmc^2, where Qdot is cooling power in microwatts, n3dot is the circulated helium-3 molar flow rate in moles per second, and Tmc is the mixing-chamber temperature in kelvin. This quadratic dependence on temperature means cooling power falls sharply as the mixing chamber approaches base temperature, which is why Millidyne specifies its cooling-power figures at a fixed reference point, 100 millikelvin, rather than at base temperature, where the achievable cooling power approaches zero and any small parasitic heat leak dominates. Increasing n3dot raises cooling power roughly linearly at fixed temperature, but Millidyne caps circulation rate below the value that would flood the still and disrupt phase separation, since a flooded still degrades circulation efficiency faster than added flow rate compensates.

title: Stage Temperatures Across Millidyne Generations
text:
Base temperature and cooldown time have both improved across three Millidyne hardware generations, reflecting incremental refinements to the still-pumping line and mixing-chamber heat exchanger design.

| Generation | Released | Base temp (unloaded) | Cooldown time |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mk1 | 2018 | 15 mK | 34 hr |
| Mk2 | 2021 | 11 mK | 29 hr |
| Mk3 | 2024 | 8 mK | 26 hr |

Each generation's improvement traces to a specific change rather than general refinement: Mk2's gain came primarily from a redesigned still-pumping line, while Mk3's gain came from a continuous heat exchanger replacing the earlier step-exchanger design in the coldest stage.

title: Cooldown Commissioning Checklist
text:
Before a newly installed Millidyne system is certified ready for qubit measurement, field engineers follow a fixed commissioning sequence. 1. Evacuate the vacuum can and confirm base pressure below the specified leak-tightness threshold.
2. Precool the 50 K and 4 K stages using the pulse-tube cooler and verify each reaches its target temperature within the specified window.
3. Condense the helium-3/helium-4 mixture and begin circulation once the still reaches its operating temperature.
4. Monitor mixing-chamber temperature through the full cooldown, confirming it reaches base temperature within the generation's specified cooldown window.
5. Run a standard vibration-spectrum measurement at the cold plate and confirm attenuation meets the isolation-stack specification before releasing the system for qubit installation. A system failing the vibration check is re-tuned and re-measured rather than certified provisionally.

title: Qubit Coherence and Temperature Statistics
text: Across Millidyne deployments reporting coherence data, superconducting transmon qubits measured at the standard 100 millikelvin operating point show a mean energy-relaxation time T1 of 118 microseconds, dropping to a mean of 61 microseconds when the same qubits are measured at an elevated mixing-chamber temperature of 150 millikelvin. Dephasing time T2 shows a comparable sensitivity, averaging 94 microseconds at 100 millikelvin against 52 microseconds at 150 millikelvin across the same sample of 340 measured qubits. Quasiparticle-poisoning events, which degrade T1 sharply when detected, occur at a mean rate of 0.8 events per qubit per hour at base temperature, rising to 4.1 events per qubit per hour at 150 millikelvin. These figures are drawn from Millidyne's aggregated customer telemetry program, which 22 of the platform's deployed systems currently participate in.

title: International Dilution Refrigeration Research
text: Millidyne's still-pumping line design draws on dilution-refrigeration research published outside the English-language cryogenics literature most engineers reference first. Volkov and Sidorova's Оптимизация циркуляции гелия-3 в рефрижераторах растворения для криостатов сверхпроводящих кубитов (2019) established much of the circulation-rate optimization methodology Millidyne's still-pumping specification still follows. A separate line of Finnish-language research, summarized in Häkkinen's report, "Jatkuvan lämmönvaihtimen suunnittelu millikelvin-lämpötiloissa parantaa jäähdytystehoa merkittävästi," directly informed the continuous heat exchanger introduced in the Mk3 generation. Millidyne's engineering documentation cites both works in its mixing-chamber design appendix, alongside domestic research on vibration isolation for flux-sensitive qubit designs.

title: Why Coherence Degrades With Temperature
text: Superconducting qubit coherence degrades with rising mixing-chamber temperature for two compounding reasons that Millidyne's thermal engineering treats as equally important rather than prioritizing one over the other. First, thermally excited quasiparticles, electron-like excitations that break the superconducting Cooper-pair condensate, become exponentially more numerous as temperature rises relative to the superconducting gap, and each quasiparticle that tunnels across a qubit's Josephson junction can directly cause an energy-relaxation event. Second, higher mixing-chamber temperature raises the thermal occupation of the qubit's own readout resonator and control wiring, injecting additional dephasing noise even before any quasiparticle-poisoning event occurs. Because both effects worsen together as temperature rises, Millidyne's specification treats mixing-chamber temperature as the single most consequential lever available to a customer for improving measured coherence, ahead of wiring quality or shielding, though both of those remain necessary rather than optional.

title: Vibration Isolation Performance by Configuration
text:
Residual vibration at the cold plate varies by isolation-stack configuration, measured as peak velocity at the pulse-tube's fundamental frequency.

| Configuration | Peak velocity at 1.4 Hz | Coherence impact (T2) |
|---|---|---|
| No isolation stack | 340 nm/s | -18% |
| Standard stack | 42 nm/s | -3% |
| Extended stack | 9 nm/s | <1% |

The extended stack, which adds a second spring-and-mass stage beyond the standard configuration, is recommended specifically for flux-tunable qubit designs, which Millidyne's engineering team has found measurably more vibration-sensitive than fixed-frequency transmon designs at equivalent residual velocity.

title: Deployment Statistics
text: Millidyne has shipped 97 systems since the Mk1 generation's 2018 release, of which 71 remain in active operation as of the most recent 2026 deployment survey. Average system uptime, measured as the fraction of scheduled operating time the mixing chamber spends at or below its qualification temperature, is 94.6% across the fleet, with unplanned warmups accounting for the majority of downtime. The largest single customer deployment operates 6 Millidyne systems in parallel supporting a 400-plus-qubit processor program. Mean time between unplanned warmups is 2,100 hours fleet-wide, though systems on the high-density wiring kit report a mean of 2,600 hours, consistent with that kit's improved thermal margin reducing the incidence of load-related warmup events.

title: Quasiparticle Density Versus Temperature
text: Quasiparticle density in the superconducting films forming Millidyne-hosted qubits follows the standard thermal-equilibrium relation nqp ∝ sqrt(T) * exp(-Delta / (kB * T)), where Delta is the superconducting energy gap, kB is Boltzmann's constant, and T is the effective electron temperature at the qubit. This exponential dependence means a modest rise in effective temperature, well short of the bulk mixing-chamber temperature itself, can still measurably raise quasiparticle density if wiring or shielding allows external noise to heat the qubit's local electron bath above the phonon bath it nominally shares with the mixing chamber. Millidyne's specification therefore distinguishes mixing-chamber temperature from qubit effective temperature explicitly, since the two frequently diverge by tens of millikelvin in a poorly shielded installation even when the mixing-chamber thermometer itself reads well within specification.

title: International Qubit Shielding Research
text: Millidyne's shielding recommendations draw on research addressing exactly this mixing-chamber-versus-effective-temperature gap, published in venues outside the usual English-language superconducting-qubit literature. Kim and Park's 초전도 큐비트의 유효 전자 온도와 혼합실 온도 사이의 괴리에 관한 연구 (2020) quantified the divergence between electron temperature and phonon-bath temperature under realistic wiring conditions, work Millidyne's shielding-kit specification cites directly. A complementary Italian-language study, Bianchi's "La schermatura a infrarossi riduce sensibilmente la densità di quasiparticelle nei qubit superconduttori," established the infrared-shielding approach Millidyne's high-density wiring kit incorporates at each thermalization stage. Both works appear in Millidyne's shielding-kit engineering appendix alongside its own internally measured quasiparticle-poisoning telemetry.

title: Manufacturing and Cost Statistics
text: A standard Millidyne Mk3 system, configured with the 256-line wiring harness, has a list price of 1.42 million dollars as of the 2026 price sheet, with the high-density wiring kit adding a further 180,000 dollars. Manufacturing lead time from order to shipment averages 22 weeks, dominated by an 11-week lead time on the dilution unit's custom heat exchanger, sourced from a single specialized supplier. Field installation and commissioning typically requires 9 working days on site, plus the 26-hour cooldown itself. Warranty claims related to the dilution unit specifically, as opposed to the pulse-tube precooler or vibration stack, occurred at a rate of 4.2% of shipped units within the first year of operation across the Mk2 and Mk3 generations combined.

title: Field Calibration Procedure
text:
Field engineers recalibrate a Millidyne system's thermometry and cooling-power figures on a fixed annual schedule, or immediately following any unplanned warmup exceeding 48 hours above the still's operating temperature. 1. Verify all cold-stage thermometers against a calibrated reference sensor traceable to a primary temperature standard.
2. Measure mixing-chamber cooling power at the 100 millikelvin reference point and compare against the generation's specification curve.
3. Re-run the standard vibration-spectrum measurement and compare against the isolation stack's baseline measurement taken at installation.
4. Cross-check helium-3 circulation rate against the still-pumping line's rated flow to detect early degradation.
5. Log all measurements to the customer's maintenance record and flag any figure outside a 10% tolerance band for follow-up service. A system whose cooling power has drifted outside tolerance is scheduled for heat-exchanger inspection before recertification.

title: Incident Postmortem: The 2023 Mixing-Chamber Contamination Event
text: In August 2023, a Millidyne Mk2 system operated by a university quantum-computing group experienced a sudden, unexplained rise in mixing-chamber base temperature, from a qualified 11 millikelvin to a stabilized 34 millikelvin, over the course of roughly six hours, well outside the drift the system's maintenance history had ever shown. The customer's operations team initially suspected a vibration-isolation failure, since a degraded isolation stack was the most common cause of gradual coherence loss in their prior experience, but a vibration-spectrum measurement showed no meaningful change from the system's baseline. Millidyne's field support team was engaged after the customer's own troubleshooting ruled out the obvious candidates, and a remote review of the system's telemetry showed still-stage pressure had drifted upward gradually over the preceding three weeks before the sudden mixing-chamber temperature rise, a pattern the customer's own monitoring had not flagged since no single reading crossed an alert threshold on its own. On-site inspection, requiring a full warmup and disassembly of the dilution unit, found a small quantity of frozen air contamination lodged in the mixing-chamber heat exchanger's finest channels, most likely introduced through a slow vacuum leak in a seal that had degraded gradually rather than failing suddenly. The contamination partially blocked helium-3 circulation through the exchanger's narrowest passages, reducing effective cooling power without producing any single dramatic symptom until the blockage crossed a threshold where circulation could no longer sustain the qualified base temperature. The seal was replaced, the heat exchanger cleaned and re-tested, and the system returned to specification within two weeks of the initial temperature rise being reported. The postmortem's central finding was that gradual still-pressure drift, while individually within each reading's own alert threshold, should have been flagged as a trend rather than evaluated only against a fixed instantaneous threshold. Millidyne's monitoring software was updated in the following release to flag sustained multi-week pressure trends explicitly, a change that has since caught two comparable slow leaks at other customer sites before either progressed to a full mixing-chamber temperature excursion, according to the field support team's most recent incident log. A secondary finding concerned the customer's troubleshooting sequence: because vibration-isolation failure was the most common cause of coherence loss the operations team had previously encountered, they spent nearly two days ruling it out before escalating, time better spent reviewing the still-pressure trend directly. Millidyne's revised troubleshooting guide now recommends checking pressure-trend history before vibration measurements for any unexplained base-temperature rise, reordering the diagnostic sequence based on this incident's timeline. The system has since operated without a comparable excursion, and the case is now used as a training example for engineers learning to distinguish a genuine hardware fault from slow seal degradation no single reading would have flagged.

title: FAQ: Base Temperature
text:
Q: What base temperature does a standard Millidyne system reach?
A: Millidyne's standard configuration uses four cooling stages above the mixing chamber: a 50 K stage, a 4 K stage, a still stage held at 0.7 K, and a cold plate at approximately 0.1 K, with the mixing chamber itself reaching a base temperature of 6 millikelvin unloaded. This unloaded figure represents the coldest achievable reading under ideal conditions and is distinct from the qualified operating temperature customers see once a full wiring harness and qubit sample are installed, which typically runs several millikelvin warmer due to the added heat load. Customers evaluating Millidyne against a competing dilution-refrigerator vendor are encouraged to compare loaded, wired-system figures rather than unloaded base-temperature specifications, since the gap between the two varies considerably across vendors depending on wiring and thermalization design.

title: Support Ticket Resolution: Case 4471
text: Case 4471, closed in February 2026, was opened by a customer questioning whether their site's unplanned-warmup frequency was unusual compared to the rest of the installed fleet. Support engineers pulled the customer's telemetry history alongside fleet-wide figures for comparison, citing the deployment survey directly rather than recomputing from raw telemetry: mean time between unplanned warmups is 1,700 hours fleet-wide, though systems on the high-density wiring kit report a mean of 2,600 hours, consistent with that kit's improved thermal margin reducing the incidence of load-related warmup events. The customer's own site measured 1,640 hours between warmups over the prior year, close enough to the fleet figure that support closed the case without recommending any hardware inspection, noting the difference fell well within normal site-to-site variation.
